Hardcoded values found
- No hardcoded numeric constants in these component classes that are relevant to the stacked/unstacked coordinate merging problem.

3b) Parent class of DataArray(s)
- DataArray inherits: AbstractArray, DataWithCoords (I already opened xarray/core/common.py)
  - AbstractArray methods I inspected include: __bool__, __float__, __int__, __complex__, __array__, __repr__, _repr_html_, _iter, __iter__, get_axis_num/_get_axis_num, sizes property, and the array reduce injection mechanism. DataArray overrides some of these for its specialized behavior (most of the heavy-lifting methods are defined in DataArray itself).
  - DataWithCoords methods include: squeeze, get_index, _calc_assign_results, assign_coords, reset_coords, assign_attrs and many utility helpers (these are used by DataArray, and DataArray often delegates to its dataset round-trip methods).
- Which parent methods are overridden by DataArray?
  - DataWithCoords.squeeze -> DataArray uses DataWithCoords.squeeze; DataArray defines its own isel/sel/drop methods which underlie squeeze. DataArray implements many methods (sel/isec/etc.) that are not in parent or that wrap into dataset roundtrips. For our purposes the important ones are:
    - get_index (DataWithCoords) is used by to_unstacked_dataset via self.indexes[dim] (via DataArray.indexes property).
    - squeeze (DataWithCoords) is used by DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset (it calls squeeze(drop=True) on selections).
  - DataArray overrides many higher-level behaviors but nothing in parent assumes keeping a stacked coordinate alive as a coordinate after selecting and squeezing.

3c) Sibling classes inheriting from the same parent
- Dataset shares DataWithCoords too; Dataset has more complex merging/stack/unstack logic. I inspected Dataset.to_stacked_array and Dataset.unstack; they follow consistent patterns (stack creates MultiIndex coordinate new_dim on the DataArray, unstack splits MultiIndex to new dims). The correct inverse of to_stacked_array is Dataset.to_stacked_array followed by DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset. Tests in xarray/tests/test_dataset.py show an intended roundtrip for mixed-dimensional variables (and pass), but the MCVE shows an edge-case when variables are single-dimensional causing MergeError.

3d) Call graph / relations
- Dataset.to_stacked_array constructs a DataArray with a new MultiIndex coordinate; DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset is supposed to invert that by selecting along variable level and returning a Dataset of DataArrays. In the faulty case, it hands DataArray objects with the stacked coordinate still attached to Dataset.__init__, which tries to merge their coordinates but finds conflicting index objects for that coordinate and raises MergeError.

Checkpoint 4 — Propose a fix, then immediately challenge it

Proposed fix (concise)
- Modify DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset so that after selecting and squeezing each per-variable DataArray, we drop the stacked coordinate (the coordinate named dim) from that DataArray before putting it into the data_dict. Concretely, change:

    for k in variables:
        data_dict[k] = self.sel({variable_dim: k}).squeeze(drop=True)

  to:

    for k in variables:
        da = self.sel({variable_dim: k}).squeeze(drop=True)
        # remove the stacked MultiIndex coordinate to avoid coordinate conflicts
        # when constructing the unstacked Dataset
        if dim in da.coords:
            da = da.drop_vars(dim, errors="ignore")
        data_dict[k] = da

- This removes the stacked MultiIndex coordinate from each selected DataArray, so Dataset(data_dict) will not attempt to merge conflicting 'dim' coordinates.

Patch location
- xarray/core/dataarray.py: method DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset (currently lines ~1953–1967). Only this method needs to change.

Why this fixes the bug
- The MergeError occurs because the selected DataArrays contain a coordinate named after the stacked dimension (a MultiIndex) whose underlying Index objects differ between per-variable slices. Dropping that stacked coordinate before constructing a Dataset avoids the merge-time conflict. The intended semantics of to_unstacked_dataset is to expand a MultiIndex level to dataset variables and keep remaining levels as coordinates/dimensions; keeping the original stacked MultiIndex coordinate (the entire dimensional MultiIndex) in each variable is unnecessary and causes the merging problem. Dropping the stacked coordinate is correct and consistent with the intended result.

Now answer all required challenge questions referencing inspected code:

4a) "What assumption does my fix change?"
- The current code assumes that it's safe to hand per-variable DataArrays (resulting from sel(...).squeeze()) to Dataset(...) without removing the stacked MultiIndex coordinate 'dim'. My fix changes that assumption: it asserts that the stacked coordinate 'dim' should not remain as a coordinate on the per-variable DataArrays when re-constructing the unstacked Dataset.

4b) "What hardcoded values encode this assumption?"
- There are no numeric hardcoded constants relevant to this assumption in the files we inspected. The issue is a logical one (presence of the coordinate named dim in da.coords) rather than a numeric assumption. The relevant hardcoded strings/identifiers are:
  - The packed usage of dim and variable_dim names when creating/using the MultiIndex in Dataset.to_stacked_array (e.g., new_dim name, variable_dim default "variable").
  - In Dataset.to_stacked_array ensure_stackable, assign_coords[dim] = None is used to set missing dims -> this causes NaN positions in the MultiIndex for missing levels. These are not numeric constants but play into how the stacked MultiIndex is formed. If we remove the stacked MultiIndex from the per-variable DataArrays on unstacking, these are no longer problematic.

Assess each possible hardcoded value from component classes:
- Dataset.to_stacked_array uses variable_dim="variable" by default. This is not changed; the fix only uses the provided dim argument to drop the stacked coordinate.
- None/NaN as placeholder for missing coordinates used by ensure_stackable is unchanged. Dropping the stacked coordinate makes the subsequent Dataset construction ignore those placeholders.

Conclusion: No numeric hardcoded constants must be made dynamic for this fix.

4c) "What about the reverse operation?"
- The reverse operation is Dataset.to_stacked_array -> DataArray (stacked), and DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset should be its inverse.
- Dataset.to_stacked_array builds the stacked MultiIndex and attaches it as a coordinate named new_dim (data_array[new_dim] = IndexVariable(new_dim, new_idx)). The reverse operation currently (before fix) calls sel and squeeze and returns Dataset(...) of the extracted arrays, but because the stacked coordinate remains attached it causes merges to fail.
- My proposed fix removes the stacked MultiIndex coordinate 'dim' from each per-variable DataArray so Dataset(data_dict) receives clean DataArrays with the expected original coordinates and no conflicting new_dim coordinate. That restores the roundtrip:

Concrete substitution / trace for the MCVE:

- Given original:
    arr = DataArray(np.arange(3), coords=[('x', [0,1,2])])  # dims ('x',)
    data = Dataset({'a': arr, 'b': arr})
    stacked = data.to_stacked_array('y', sample_dims=['x'])  # stacked dims: ('x','y') where y is MultiIndex over variable
- stacked.indexes['y'] is a pd.MultiIndex with levels[0]=['a','b'], names[0]='variable'.
- Call stacked.to_unstacked_dataset('y'):
  - idx = self.indexes['y'] (MultiIndex)
  - level_number = idx._get_level_number(level=0) -> 0
  - variables = idx.levels[0] -> Index(['a','b'])
  - variable_dim = idx.names[0] -> 'variable'
  - For k in ['a','b']:
    - self.sel({'variable':k}) selects the subarray along the 'variable' level -> returns DataArray with a y-dimension length 1 (the selected MultiIndex entry)
    - .squeeze(drop=True) removes the length 1 y-dimension -> returns DataArray dims ('x',) but coords likely still include the original stacked coordinate 'y' as a coordinate (IndexVariable) with different internal index objects per selection.
    - Dataset({...}) tries to merge those coords and finds conflicting values for 'y' -> MergeError.
- After fix:
  - After squeeze, drop the coordinate named 'y' (the stacked coordinate). The per-variable DataArray has only the original coords (x). Dataset(data_dict) now sees two DataArrays with same x coordinate, no conflicting 'y' coordinate -> merge succeeds and roundtrip returns expected Dataset.

Do we need to change Dataset.to_stacked_array or other methods?
- No. Dataset.to_stacked_array already constructs the stacked MultiIndex in the stacked DataArray. The correct inverse is that unstacking returns a Dataset with variables corresponding to MultiIndex level entries and without the stacked MultiIndex coordinate attached to each variable. Dropping the stacked coordinate on the unstack side is the correct approach.

4d) "Does every existing method still work?"
- DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset is changed so the per-variable DataArray has the stacked coordinate removed if present. This should not break other DataArray methods because:
  - DataArray.drop_vars exists and returns DataArray; using errors='ignore' avoids errors if the stack coordinate is not present.
  - Other methods that rely on the stacked coordinate being present only operate on stacked DataArray objects; after to_unstacked_dataset has created a Dataset, it returns out of DataArray context. We are not changing other methods’ behavior.
- Verify methods that call to_unstacked_dataset:
  - Tests in test_dataset.py call .to_unstacked_dataset and expect roundtrip to Dataset. The test test_to_stacked_array_to_unstacked_dataset and test_to_stacked_array_to_unstacked_dataset_different_dimension should pass with the fix (the MCVE that failed will now succeed).
- Other code using sel(...).squeeze(...).drop_vars(...) is safe.

4e) "Does any NEW method need to be added?"
- No new methods are necessary. The fix uses existing DataArray APIs:
  - sel, squeeze, drop_vars (which calls into Dataset.drop_vars via _to_temp_dataset/_from_temp_dataset).
- No parent method needs overriding. Specifically, parent class methods that perform complementary operations (like DataArray._to_temp_dataset and Dataset.__init__ merging) continue to work; we just ensure the inputs to Dataset.__init__ are clean (i.e., no conflicting stacked coordinate).

Checkpoint 5 — Final report (complete analysis and fix)

Root cause
- DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset collected per-variable DataArrays by selecting the requested MultiIndex level and squeezing out the stacked dimension, but it left the original stacked coordinate (the MultiIndex coordinate with name dim) attached to each per-variable DataArray. When these per-variable DataArrays are passed to Dataset(data_dict) for construction, Dataset.__init__ attempts to merge coordinates across inputs, and finds conflicting values for the stacked coordinate (different Index objects), raising MergeError. This is especially exposed when variables have only a single non-sample dimension (the stacked MultiIndex contains only one level), as in the user’s MCVE.

Every location that needs to change
- Single change required:
  - xarray/core/dataarray.py: DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset

What the current code does and what assumption it encodes
- Current code (excerpt):

    idx = self.indexes[dim]
    ...
    variables = idx.levels[level_number]
    variable_dim = idx.names[level_number]

    data_dict = {}
    for k in variables:
        data_dict[k] = self.sel({variable_dim: k}).squeeze(drop=True)

    return Dataset(data_dict)

- Assumption encoded (implicit): after sel({variable_dim: k}).squeeze(drop=True) the resulting DataArray can be used directly as a value in the Dataset constructor, i.e., it has coordinates that will merge cleanly across variables. That assumption fails because the stacked coordinate (dim) is left as a coordinate in each DataArray, and these differ between variables.

What the fix should be (concrete)
- Remove the stacked MultiIndex coordinate (the coordinate named dim) from each selected DataArray before placing it into data_dict. Use DataArray.drop_vars(..., errors="ignore") for safety.

Exact patch (conceptual diff)

In xarray/core/dataarray.py, in DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset:

Replace:

    # pull variables out of datarray
    data_dict = {}
    for k in variables:
        data_dict[k] = self.sel({variable_dim: k}).squeeze(drop=True)

    # unstacked dataset
    return Dataset(data_dict)

With:

    # pull variables out of datarray
    data_dict = {}
    for k in variables:
        da = self.sel({variable_dim: k}).squeeze(drop=True)
        # drop the stacked coordinate (the MultiIndex for `dim`) from each
        # selected DataArray before constructing a Dataset to avoid merge
        # conflicts on that coordinate.
        # errors='ignore' used so we do nothing if the coordinate is absent.
        da = da.drop_vars(dim, errors="ignore")
        data_dict[k] = da

    # unstacked dataset
    return Dataset(data_dict)

Notes
- Using errors="ignore" ensures that if the coordinate is not present (for some reason), no error is raised.
- We only remove the coordinate named dim, which is the stacked coordinate name, not any of the other level coordinates that should remain in the unstacked variables (e.g., other MultiIndex levels are typically promoted to coordinates/dimensions by Dataset.unstack or other code paths).

Any other locations to change?
- I reviewed Dataset.to_stacked_array and Dataset.unstack and merge.py; they do not require changes. They produce a MultiIndex coordinate and the unstack path needs to remove the stacked MultiIndex when expanding to variables. No further code modifications are required.

Why this is a complete fix
- The MergeError arises during Dataset construction when merging the stacked coordinate across the per-variable DataArrays; removing that coordinate removes the source of conflict. This addresses the failing MCVE and maintains the expected roundtrip behavior for stack/unstacking. The fix does not break other behaviors because:
  - It only drops the stacked coordinate on the per-variable DataArrays being put into the Dataset (the stacked MultiIndex is still present for the stacked DataArray until to_unstacked_dataset is called).
  - Existing tests that exercise to_stacked_array -> to_unstacked_dataset roundtrip (including different-dimension variables) remain valid and should pass.

Suggested test to add / update
- Add a test reproducing the MCVE to ensure the roundtrip works for single-dimension variables:
  - The test in xarray/tests/test_dataset.py already has similar cases, but ensure a case like:

    def test_to_stacked_array_to_unstacked_dataset_single_dim():
        arr = xr.DataArray(np.arange(3), coords=[("x", [0, 1, 2])])
        data = xr.Dataset({"a": arr, "b": arr})
        stacked = data.to_stacked_array("y", sample_dims=["x"])
        roundtripped = stacked.to_unstacked_dataset("y")
        assert_identical(data, roundtripped)

- This will capture the previously failing MCVE.

Summary of the fix
- Single-line logic change: when constructing the dict of variables in DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset, drop the stacked coordinate dim from each selected DataArray before passing them to Dataset(). This prevents MergeError conflicts on the stacked coordinate and restores the expected roundtrip behavior.

If you want, I can:
- Prepare the exact patch (git diff) for xarray/core/dataarray.py.
- Run through the unit tests that exercise stack/unstack and to_stacked_array/to_unstacked_dataset to confirm the change (I can't run tests here, but I can indicate the tests that should be run).
compressed_analysis:
===HIGH_LEVEL===

- Bug: DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset fails to roundtrip Dataset.to_stacked_array when the stacked coordinate has only a single level (user MCVE). The wrong assumption is that per-variable DataArray objects produced by sel(...).squeeze() can be handed directly to Dataset(...) without removing the stacked MultiIndex coordinate.
- Root cause (where it lives): xarray/core/dataarray.py, class DataArray, method to_unstacked_dataset — it leaves the stacked MultiIndex coordinate (the coordinate named dim) attached to every selected DataArray, producing conflicting coordinates during Dataset construction and raising MergeError.
- Fix strategy: after selecting each variable (self.sel(...).squeeze(drop=True)) drop the stacked coordinate named dim from the resulting DataArray before inserting it into data_dict. This avoids Dataset.merge conflicts. Only DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset needs to change.
- Hardcoded values/constants: none numeric need changing. The fix uses existing APIs; it does not alter Dataset.to_stacked_array or change the default variable_dim ("variable").
- New methods/overrides: none required. Use the existing DataArray.drop_vars(..., errors="ignore") API.
- Investigation gap: I did not run test execution here; the change should be covered by adding the MCVE as a unit test. Also, I did not inspect every possible edge-case where an original dataset had an actual coordinate with the same name as the stacked-dimension name (rare but possible); the chosen behavior (drop stacked dim coordinate only) is consistent with the intended unstack semantics, but one should add a test for name collisions between original coords and stacked-dimension name.

===MID_LEVEL===

### Finding 1: Stacked coordinate retained on per-variable selections (root cause)
What: DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset leaves the stacked MultiIndex coordinate (name = dim passed into to_unstacked_dataset) on each sel(...).squeeze(...) result, so Dataset(data_dict) must merge that coordinate across inputs and fails with MergeError.  
Where: xarray/core/dataarray.py : DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset.  
Why it matters: The merge step sees conflicting pd.Index/pd.MultiIndex objects for the same coordinate name (e.g., 'y') and raises MergeError. (Found in Checkpoint 1 & 2.)

### Finding 2: How the stacked MultiIndex is constructed (relevant context)
What: Dataset.to_stacked_array builds a MultiIndex and assigns it as an IndexVariable on the returned DataArray: data_array[new_dim] = IndexVariable(new_dim, new_idx). Missing stacking levels are filled with None/NaN placeholders in ensure_stackable.  
Where: xarray/core/dataset.py : Dataset.to_stacked_array (ensure_stackable and lines ~3478–3483).  
Why it matters: explains why the stacked coordinate exists and why per-variable slices can have incompatible index objects (Found in Checkpoint 1).

### Finding 3: MergeError originates from Dataset constructor merging coords
What: Dataset.__init__ calls merge_data_and_coords -> merge_core; conflicting coordinate values same-name across inputs produce MergeError with message matching the MCVE.  
Where: xarray/core/dataset.py : Dataset.__init__ -> merge_data_and_coords (xarray/core/merge.py).  
Why it matters: confirms the failure mode — conflicts happen during merge of coordinates from the per-variable DataArrays (Found in Checkpoint 1).

### Finding 4: Minimal and safe fix is to drop stacked coordinate before merge
What: Dropping the stacked coordinate (named dim) from each selected DataArray prevents coordinate merge conflicts and restores the intended unstacked Dataset contents. Use da = da.drop_vars(dim, errors="ignore") after sel(...).squeeze(...).  
Where to change: xarray/core/dataarray.py : DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset (Found in Checkpoints 1–4).

### Finding 5: No other code must change for correctness
What: Dataset.to_stacked_array, Dataset.unstack, and merge logic are correct; the problem is only that to_unstacked_dataset hands conflicting coordinates to Dataset constructor. Changing DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset to drop the stacked coordinate addresses the root cause without additional edits. (Found in Checkpoint 3 & 4.)

### Finding 6: Missing test for MCVE (coverage gap)
What: Add a test that reproduces the MCVE: stacking a Dataset of single-dim variables and roundtripping via to_stacked_array(...).to_unstacked_dataset(...) must equal the original Dataset.  
Where: xarray/tests/test_dataset.py (add test near other to_stacked_array tests). (Found in Checkpoint 4e.)

===LOW_LEVEL===

### Location: xarray/core/dataarray.py:DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset
- **Wrong assumption**: Assumes sel({variable_dim: k}).squeeze(drop=True) produces DataArray objects that can be merged into a Dataset without coordinate conflicts (i.e., that no stacked coordinate named dim remains attached).
- **Evidence**: Current implementation code (excerpt) is:

    idx = self.indexes[dim]
    level_number = idx._get_level_number(level)
    variables = idx.levels[level_number]
    variable_dim = idx.names[level_number]

    data_dict = {}
    for k in variables:
        data_dict[k] = self.sel({variable_dim: k}).squeeze(drop=True)

    return Dataset(data_dict)

  This leaves the stacked MultiIndex coordinate (the IndexVariable set by Dataset.to_stacked_array at data_array[new_dim] = IndexVariable(new_dim, new_idx)) attached to each per-variable DataArray. When Dataset(data_dict) runs, merge_data_and_coords attempts to merge that same coordinate name across the dict entries and raises MergeError: "conflicting values for variable 'y' on objects to be combined..." (matches user MCVE). (Found in Checkpoints 1 & 2.)
- **Related locations**: xarray/core/dataset.py:Dataset.to_stacked_array (builds the MultiIndex and assigns it), xarray/core/merge.py:merge_data_and_coords and merge_core (raise MergeError during merging).

### Location: xarray/core/dataset.py:Dataset.to_stacked_array
- **Wrong assumption (precondition for unstack)**: Constructs stacked DataArray with data_array[new_dim] = IndexVariable(new_dim, new_idx) and relies on inverse operation to remove this stacked coordinate when expanding to dataset variables. The code does not itself remove it (unstack logic on Dataset works differently).  
- **Evidence**: Code excerpt:

    idx = data_array.indexes[new_dim]
    levels = [idx.levels[0]] + [
        level.astype(self[level.name].dtype) for level in idx.levels[1:]
    ]
    new_idx = idx.set_levels(levels)
    data_array[new_dim] = IndexVariable(new_dim, new_idx)

  This produces the coordinate that later causes conflicts if not removed on unstack. (Found in Checkpoint 1.)
- **Related locations**: xarray/core/dataarray.py:DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset (must remove this coordinate), xarray/tests/test_dataset.py (tests exercising roundtrip).

### Location: xarray/core/merge.py:merge_core / merge_data_and_coords
- **Wrong assumption**: Merge code is correct; it assumes inputs intended for Dataset construction will not carry conflicting coordinate variable objects for the same coordinate name. When that assumption is violated (stacked coordinate retained across per-variable DataArrays), it correctly raises MergeError.
- **Evidence**: merge_data_and_coords constructs merged variables and raises MergeError when conflicts are found; user error message matches merge's conflict message. (Found in Checkpoint 1.)
- **Related locations**: xarray/core/dataarray.py:DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset (the caller that must ensure inputs don't contain conflicting coordinates).

### Precise code change to apply (exact lines to replace)
- Replace in xarray/core/dataarray.py (inside DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset):

Old fragment:

    data_dict = {}
    for k in variables:
        data_dict[k] = self.sel({variable_dim: k}).squeeze(drop=True)

New fragment:

    data_dict = {}
    for k in variables:
        da = self.sel({variable_dim: k}).squeeze(drop=True)
        # remove the stacked MultiIndex coordinate before building Dataset to
        # avoid merge conflicts on that coordinate
        da = da.drop_vars(dim, errors="ignore")
        data_dict[k] = da

- Keep all other logic intact.

### Tests to add (exact MCVE)
- Add test (pseudocode) to xarray/tests/test_dataset.py near other to_stacked_array tests:

    def test_to_stacked_array_to_unstacked_dataset_single_dim():
        arr = xr.DataArray(np.arange(3), coords=[("x", [0, 1, 2])])
        data = xr.Dataset({"a": arr, "b": arr})
        stacked = data.to_stacked_array("y", sample_dims=["x"])
        roundtripped = stacked.to_unstacked_dataset("y")
        assert_identical(data, roundtripped)

This guards against regressions of the MCVE.

--- End of report.

Thought: Phase 1 — Verification and completeness check

Summary of what I checked and why
- I inspected the implementations of:
  - DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset (xarray/core/dataarray.py) to confirm whether it removes the stacked coordinate when producing per-variable DataArray objects.
  - Dataset.to_stacked_array (xarray/core/dataset.py) to confirm how the stacked MultiIndex coordinate is created and attached.
  - The merge logic (xarray/core/merge.py) and Dataset.__init__ (xarray/core/dataset.py) to confirm the point at which a MergeError would be raised when multiple inputs carry conflicting coordinates.
- My expectation: to_unstacked_dataset should not leave the stacked coordinate attached on per-variable results; if it does, Dataset construction (merge) may see same-named coords on several inputs and raise MergeError. I looked for concrete code lines that support or contradict these claims.

What I ran and what I expected to learn (reasoning written before tool calls)
- I searched for to_unstacked_dataset to locate its implementation and tests, to confirm the draft's behavior claims and whether tests already cover the failure.
- I opened the DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset implementation to confirm the exact loop that constructs the data_dict and whether it drops the stacked coordinate.
- I opened Dataset.to_stacked_array to confirm whether it attaches an IndexVariable named new_dim to the stacked DataArray.
- I opened merge.py and Dataset.__init__ to confirm error message and merge behavior.

Results and analysis (based on the code I retrieved)

1) DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset (xarray/core/dataarray.py)
- I inspected the implementation. The method begins at line 1901 and the relevant fragment is:

    idx = self.indexes[dim]
    ...
    level_number = idx._get_level_number(level)
    variables = idx.levels[level_number]
    variable_dim = idx.names[level_number]

    # pull variables out of datarray
    data_dict = {}
    for k in variables:
        data_dict[k] = self.sel({variable_dim: k}).squeeze(drop=True)

    # unstacked dataset
    return Dataset(data_dict)

  (lines 1953–1967 in the file).
- Analysis: to_unstacked_dataset iterates over the values in the selected MultiIndex level and for each value selects a slice via self.sel({variable_dim: k}).squeeze(drop=True) and inserts that DataArray directly into data_dict. There is no code here that explicitly drops the stacked coordinate named by dim (the method argument). Therefore the method does not remove any coordinate named dim from the per-variable DataArray objects it creates.

2) Dataset.to_stacked_array (xarray/core/dataset.py)
- I inspected the implementation. After concatenation the method coerces MultiIndex levels and then assigns an IndexVariable as a coordinate with the new_dim name:

    idx = data_array.indexes[new_dim]
    levels = [idx.levels[0]] + [
        level.astype(self[level.name].dtype) for level in idx.levels[1:]
    ]
    new_idx = idx.set_levels(levels)
    data_array[new_dim] = IndexVariable(new_dim, new_idx)

  (lines 3478–3483).
- Analysis: to_stacked_array attaches an IndexVariable with name new_dim (e.g., 'y') to the resulting DataArray. That is the stacked MultiIndex coordinate the unstacking code later inspects.

3) Merge behavior and MergeError (xarray/core/merge.py and Dataset.__init__)
- Dataset.__init__ calls merge_data_and_coords(data_vars, coords, compat="broadcast_equals") (lines 543–545).
- merge_data_and_coords delegates to merge_core (around line 461).
- In merge logic, conflicts are detected and a MergeError is raised with the message:

    f"conflicting values for variable {name!r} on objects to be combined. "
    "You can skip this check by specifying compat='override'."

  (merge.py around lines 141–145).
- Analysis: Dataset construction merges variables and coordinates from the provided mappings; when same-named coordinate variables coming from different inputs are incompatible under the chosen compat, merge_core raises the MergeError observed in the MCVE.

4) Do the code facts support the draft report claims?
- The draft claim that to_unstacked_dataset leaves the stacked MultiIndex coordinate attached is supported: the to_unstacked_dataset implementation constructs per-variable DataArrays via sel(...).squeeze(...) and does not drop a coordinate named dim.
- The draft claim that the stacked coordinate is created as an IndexVariable by to_stacked_array is supported by the code assignment data_array[new_dim] = IndexVariable(new_dim, new_idx).
- The claim that Dataset construction detects conflicting same-named coordinate objects and raises MergeError is supported by merge.py raising that exact message when variables differ.
- I did not need to inspect DataArray.sel internals to confirm the diagnosis: the absence of any drop or removal in to_unstacked_dataset plus the observed MergeError are sufficient to conclude per-variable DataArrays still carry the stacked coordinate (this matches the MCVE and the merge error).

5) Completeness: secondary findings from the investigation
- The investigation noted tests exist for to_stacked_array -> to_unstacked_dataset roundtrips but the existing tests do not appear to include the exact MCVE where all variables are single-dimension (both variables 1D and stacking dims empty). I inspected existing tests:
  - test_to_stacked_array_to_unstacked_dataset (xarray/tests/test_dataset.py) exercises a 2D dataset roundtrip (lines 3033–3041).
  - test_to_stacked_array_to_unstacked_dataset_different_dimension (lines 3048–3056) tests mixed dimensionality (one variable 2D, one 1D), and that test passes in the repository.
- The MCVE differs in that both variables are single-dim; the existing tests do not explicitly cover the case where stacking_dims is empty and all variables are single-dimensional (the scenario that triggers this MergeError). This is a coverage gap (not a code bug by itself) but relevant for reproducing and preventing regressions.

6) Impact completeness check
- The critical shared state is the presence of the stacked coordinate named dim attached to the DataArray returned by to_stacked_array and retained on each per-variable selection created by to_unstacked_dataset. Any code path that constructs a Dataset from a dict of DataArray objects will go through merge_data_and_coords and can be affected if inputs carry conflicting same-named coordinates.
- I looked for other code that manipulates the same coordinate name; to_stacked_array and to_unstacked_dataset are the primary pair directly involved. The merge logic is intentionally strict about differing variable values; this is expected behavior when inputs carry conflicting coordinates.
- The investigation suggested the problem is localized to DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset leaving the stacked coordinate in place. The code inspection confirms that to_unstacked_dataset does not drop the stacked coordinate, so the root cause localization is supported.

7) Field-discipline issues in the draft
- The draft RootCause describes incorrect assumptions and lists the relevant code locations. That is appropriate.
- The investigation analysis included a concrete code change and a test; these are implementation suggestions. The final report must be a pure diagnosis (no fixes). The draft report JSON (the one you provided) did not include the explicit code-change snippet in the JSON fields, but the investigation analysis did. For the revised report I will remove prescriptive language (e.g., "Fix strategy", "Precise code change") and keep only diagnostic statements about what is wrong and where.
- The ExpectedBehavior/ObservedBehavior/Description fields in the draft are mostly correct but I will tighten them to remain user-visible and concise (no implementation-level guidance in those fields).

Conclusion of Phase 1
- Confirmed root cause and locations:
  - to_unstacked_dataset (xarray/core/dataarray.py) iterates over MultiIndex level values and constructs per-variable DataArrays with sel(...).squeeze(drop=True), but it does not remove the stacked coordinate (dim), so that coordinate remains attached to each DataArray.
  - to_stacked_array (xarray/core/dataset.py) attaches the stacked MultiIndex as an IndexVariable under the new_dim name (data_array[new_dim] = IndexVariable(...)).
  - Dataset construction merges coordinates and raises MergeError when same-named coordinate variables from different inputs are not compatible (xarray/core/merge.py).
- The investigation's additional findings (test coverage gap for all single-dim variables case) are valid and should be recorded as evidence, but they are not part of the code root cause itself.
- I will now produce the revised JSON report: keep content strictly diagnostic, include the same top-level fields as in the draft, ensure RootCause lists all relevant locations with equal weight, and do not include fix prescriptions.

bug_report.Title: DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset fails for single-dim variables (MergeError on stacked coordinate)
bug_report.Description: Calling DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset on a DataArray produced by Dataset.to_stacked_array can raise a MergeError when the stacked coordinate (the dimension passed to to_unstacked_dataset) is present but has only a single level / when variables are single-dimension. The per-variable DataArray objects constructed by to_unstacked_dataset retain the stacked MultiIndex coordinate named by the 'dim' argument, which leads to coordinate conflicts during Dataset construction.
bug_report.RootCause:
1) DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset assumes that selecting each variable with sel({variable_dim: k}).squeeze(drop=True) yields DataArray objects that do not carry the stacked coordinate named by the 'dim' argument. In reality, to_unstacked_dataset does not remove the stacked coordinate and inserts the per-variable DataArray objects into a dict which is passed directly to Dataset(...). (Location: xarray/core/dataarray.py, DataArray.to_unstacked_dataset — see loop that builds data_dict and returns Dataset(data_dict)).

2) The stacked MultiIndex coordinate is created and attached by Dataset.to_stacked_array as an IndexVariable under the new_dim name (data_array[new_dim] = IndexVariable(new_dim, new_idx)). That coordinate therefore exists on the stacked DataArray that is later unstacked. (Location: xarray/core/dataset.py, Dataset.to_stacked_array — see assignment of IndexVariable to data_array[new_dim]).

3) Dataset construction merges variables and coordinates from the provided inputs and treats same-named coordinate objects across inputs as potential conflicts; when the per-variable DataArrays still carry the same-named stacked coordinate but with incompatible index objects, merge_core/merge_data_and_coords raises a MergeError (message: "conflicting values for variable {name!r} on objects to be combined..."). (Location: xarray/core/merge.py -> merge_core / merge_data_and_coords and xarray/core/dataset.py -> Dataset.__init__ which calls merge_data_and_coords).
StepsToReproduce: import numpy as np; import xarray as xr
arr = xr.DataArray(np.arange(3), coords=[("x", [0, 1, 2])])
data = xr.Dataset({"a": arr, "b": arr})
stacked = data.to_stacked_array('y', sample_dims=['x'])
stacked.to_unstacked_dataset('y')  # raises MergeError
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: Calling to_unstacked_dataset on the result of to_stacked_array should return a Dataset equivalent to the original Dataset (a successful roundtrip) without raising an error when the intent is to expand a MultiIndex level containing variable names into data variables.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: A MergeError is raised: "conflicting values for variable 'y' on objects to be combined. You can skip this check by specifying compat='override'." This occurs because the stacked MultiIndex coordinate named 'y' remains attached to each per-variable DataArray passed into Dataset(...), producing conflicting same-named coordinate objects during the merge step.

import datetime
import functools
import warnings

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

from . import dtypes, duck_array_ops, nputils, ops
from .arithmetic import SupportsArithmetic
from .common import ImplementsArrayReduce, ImplementsDatasetReduce
from .concat import concat
from .formatting import format_array_flat
from .indexes import propagate_indexes
from .options import _get_keep_attrs
from .pycompat import integer_types
from .utils import (
    either_dict_or_kwargs,
    hashable,
    is_scalar,
    maybe_wrap_array,
    peek_at,
    safe_cast_to_index,
)
from .variable import IndexVariable, Variable, as_variable


def check_reduce_dims(reduce_dims, dimensions):

    if reduce_dims is not ...:
        if is_scalar(reduce_dims):
            reduce_dims = [reduce_dims]
        if any(dim not in dimensions for dim in reduce_dims):
            raise ValueError(
                "cannot reduce over dimensions %r. expected either '...' to reduce over all dimensions or one or more of %r."
                % (reduce_dims, dimensions)
            )


def unique_value_groups(ar, sort=True):
    """Group an array by its unique values.

    Parameters
    ----------
    ar : array-like
        Input array. This will be flattened if it is not already 1-D.
    sort : boolean, optional
        Whether or not to sort unique values.

    Returns
    -------
    values : np.ndarray
        Sorted, unique values as returned by `np.unique`.
    indices : list of lists of int
        Each element provides the integer indices in `ar` with values given by
        the corresponding value in `unique_values`.
    """
    inverse, values = pd.factorize(ar, sort=sort)
    groups = [[] for _ in range(len(values))]
    for n, g in enumerate(inverse):
        if g >= 0:
            # pandas uses -1 to mark NaN, but doesn't include them in values
            groups[g].append(n)
    return values, groups


def _dummy_copy(xarray_obj):
    from .dataarray import DataArray
    from .dataset import Dataset

    if isinstance(xarray_obj, Dataset):
        res = Dataset(
            {
                k: dtypes.get_fill_value(v.dtype)
                for k, v in xarray_obj.data_vars.items()
            },
            {
                k: dtypes.get_fill_value(v.dtype)
                for k, v in xarray_obj.coords.items()
                if k not in xarray_obj.dims
            },
            xarray_obj.attrs,
        )
    elif isinstance(xarray_obj, DataArray):
        res = DataArray(
            dtypes.get_fill_value(xarray_obj.dtype),
            {
                k: dtypes.get_fill_value(v.dtype)
                for k, v in xarray_obj.coords.items()
                if k not in xarray_obj.dims
            },
            dims=[],
            name=xarray_obj.name,
            attrs=xarray_obj.attrs,
        )
    else:  # pragma: no cover
        raise AssertionError
    return res


def _is_one_or_none(obj):
    return obj == 1 or obj is None


def _consolidate_slices(slices):
    """Consolidate adjacent slices in a list of slices.
    """
    result = []
    last_slice = slice(None)
    for slice_ in slices:
        if not isinstance(slice_, slice):
            raise ValueError("list element is not a slice: %r" % slice_)
        if (
            result
            and last_slice.stop == slice_.start
            and _is_one_or_none(last_slice.step)
            and _is_one_or_none(slice_.step)
        ):
            last_slice = slice(last_slice.start, slice_.stop, slice_.step)
            result[-1] = last_slice
        else:
            result.append(slice_)
            last_slice = slice_
    return result


def _inverse_permutation_indices(positions):
    """Like inverse_permutation, but also handles slices.

    Parameters
    ----------
    positions : list of np.ndarray or slice objects.
        If slice objects, all are assumed to be slices.

    Returns
    -------
    np.ndarray of indices or None, if no permutation is necessary.
    """
    if not positions:
        return None

    if isinstance(positions[0], slice):
        positions = _consolidate_slices(positions)
        if positions == slice(None):
            return None
        positions = [np.arange(sl.start, sl.stop, sl.step) for sl in positions]

    indices = nputils.inverse_permutation(np.concatenate(positions))
    return indices


class _DummyGroup:
    """Class for keeping track of grouped dimensions without coordinates.

    Should not be user visible.
    """

    __slots__ = ("name", "coords", "size")

    def __init__(self, obj, name, coords):
        self.name = name
        self.coords = coords
        self.size = obj.sizes[name]

    @property
    def dims(self):
        return (self.name,)

    @property
    def ndim(self):
        return 1

    @property
    def values(self):
        return range(self.size)

    @property
    def shape(self):
        return (self.size,)

    def __getitem__(self, key):
        if isinstance(key, tuple):
            key = key[0]
        return self.values[key]


def _ensure_1d(group, obj):
    if group.ndim != 1:
        # try to stack the dims of the group into a single dim
        orig_dims = group.dims
        stacked_dim = "stacked_" + "_".join(orig_dims)
        # these dimensions get created by the stack operation
        inserted_dims = [dim for dim in group.dims if dim not in group.coords]
        # the copy is necessary here, otherwise read only array raises error
        # in pandas: https://github.com/pydata/pandas/issues/12813
        group = group.stack(**{stacked_dim: orig_dims}).copy()
        obj = obj.stack(**{stacked_dim: orig_dims})
    else:
        stacked_dim = None
        inserted_dims = []
    return group, obj, stacked_dim, inserted_dims


def _unique_and_monotonic(group):
    if isinstance(group, _DummyGroup):
        return True
    else:
        index = safe_cast_to_index(group)
        return index.is_unique and index.is_monotonic


def _apply_loffset(grouper, result):
    """
    (copied from pandas)
    if loffset is set, offset the result index

    This is NOT an idempotent routine, it will be applied
    exactly once to the result.

    Parameters
    ----------
    result : Series or DataFrame
        the result of resample
    """

    needs_offset = (
        isinstance(grouper.loffset, (pd.DateOffset, datetime.timedelta))
        and isinstance(result.index, pd.DatetimeIndex)
        and len(result.index) > 0
    )

    if needs_offset:
        result.index = result.index + grouper.loffset

    grouper.loffset = None


class GroupBy(SupportsArithmetic):
    """A object that implements the split-apply-combine pattern.

    Modeled after `pandas.GroupBy`. The `GroupBy` object can be iterated over
    (unique_value, grouped_array) pairs, but the main way to interact with a
    groupby object are with the `apply` or `reduce` methods. You can also
    directly call numpy methods like `mean` or `std`.

    You should create a GroupBy object by using the `DataArray.groupby` or
    `Dataset.groupby` methods.

    See Also
    --------
    Dataset.groupby
    DataArray.groupby
    """

    __slots__ = (
        "_full_index",
        "_inserted_dims",
        "_group",
        "_group_dim",
        "_group_indices",
        "_groups",
        "_obj",
        "_restore_coord_dims",
        "_stacked_dim",
        "_unique_coord",
        "_dims",
    )

    def __init__(
        self,
        obj,
        group,
        squeeze=False,
        grouper=None,
        bins=None,
        restore_coord_dims=True,
        cut_kwargs=None,
    ):
        """Create a GroupBy object

        Parameters
        ----------
        obj : Dataset or DataArray
            Object to group.
        group : DataArray
            Array with the group values.
        squeeze : boolean, optional
            If "group" is a coordinate of object, `squeeze` controls whether
            the subarrays have a dimension of length 1 along that coordinate or
            if the dimension is squeezed out.
        grouper : pd.Grouper, optional
            Used for grouping values along the `group` array.
        bins : array-like, optional
            If `bins` is specified, the groups will be discretized into the
            specified bins by `pandas.cut`.
        restore_coord_dims : bool, default True
            If True, also restore the dimension order of multi-dimensional
            coordinates.
        cut_kwargs : dict, optional
            Extra keyword arguments to pass to `pandas.cut`

        """
        if cut_kwargs is None:
            cut_kwargs = {}
        from .dataarray import DataArray

        if grouper is not None and bins is not None:
            raise TypeError("can't specify both `grouper` and `bins`")

        if not isinstance(group, (DataArray, IndexVariable)):
            if not hashable(group):
                raise TypeError(
                    "`group` must be an xarray.DataArray or the "
                    "name of an xarray variable or dimension."
                    f"Received {group!r} instead."
                )
            group = obj[group]
            if len(group) == 0:
                raise ValueError(f"{group.name} must not be empty")

            if group.name not in obj.coords and group.name in obj.dims:
                # DummyGroups should not appear on groupby results
                group = _DummyGroup(obj, group.name, group.coords)

        if getattr(group, "name", None) is None:
            group.name = "group"

        group, obj, stacked_dim, inserted_dims = _ensure_1d(group, obj)
        (group_dim,) = group.dims

        expected_size = obj.sizes[group_dim]
        if group.size != expected_size:
            raise ValueError(
                "the group variable's length does not "
                "match the length of this variable along its "
                "dimension"
            )

        full_index = None

        if bins is not None:
            if duck_array_ops.isnull(bins).all():
                raise ValueError("All bin edges are NaN.")
            binned = pd.cut(group.values, bins, **cut_kwargs)
            new_dim_name = group.name + "_bins"
            group = DataArray(binned, group.coords, name=new_dim_name)
            full_index = binned.categories

        if grouper is not None:
            index = safe_cast_to_index(group)
            if not index.is_monotonic:
                # TODO: sort instead of raising an error
                raise ValueError("index must be monotonic for resampling")
            full_index, first_items = self._get_index_and_items(index, grouper)
            sbins = first_items.values.astype(np.int64)
            group_indices = [slice(i, j) for i, j in zip(sbins[:-1], sbins[1:])] + [
                slice(sbins[-1], None)
            ]
            unique_coord = IndexVariable(group.name, first_items.index)
        elif group.dims == (group.name,) and _unique_and_monotonic(group):
            # no need to factorize
            group_indices = np.arange(group.size)
            if not squeeze:
                # use slices to do views instead of fancy indexing
                # equivalent to: group_indices = group_indices.reshape(-1, 1)
                group_indices = [slice(i, i + 1) for i in group_indices]
            unique_coord = group
        else:
            if group.isnull().any():
                # drop any NaN valued groups.
                # also drop obj values where group was NaN
                # Use where instead of reindex to account for duplicate coordinate labels.
                obj = obj.where(group.notnull(), drop=True)
                group = group.dropna(group_dim)

            # look through group to find the unique values
            group_as_index = safe_cast_to_index(group)
            sort = bins is None and (not isinstance(group_as_index, pd.MultiIndex))
            unique_values, group_indices = unique_value_groups(
                group_as_index, sort=sort
            )
            unique_coord = IndexVariable(group.name, unique_values)

        if len(group_indices) == 0:
            if bins is not None:
                raise ValueError(
                    "None of the data falls within bins with edges %r" % bins
                )
            else:
                raise ValueError(
                    "Failed to group data. Are you grouping by a variable that is all NaN?"
                )

        # specification for the groupby operation
        self._obj = obj
        self._group = group
        self._group_dim = group_dim
        self._group_indices = group_indices
        self._unique_coord = unique_coord
        self._stacked_dim = stacked_dim
        self._inserted_dims = inserted_dims
        self._full_index = full_index
        self._restore_coord_dims = restore_coord_dims

        # cached attributes
        self._groups = None
        self._dims = None

    @property
    def dims(self):
        if self._dims is None:
            self._dims = self._obj.isel(
                **{self._group_dim: self._group_indices[0]}
            ).dims

        return self._dims

    @property
    def groups(self):
        # provided to mimic pandas.groupby
        if self._groups is None:
            self._groups = dict(zip(self._unique_coord.values, self._group_indices))
        return self._groups

    def __len__(self):
        return self._unique_coord.size

    def __iter__(self):
        return zip(self._unique_coord.values, self._iter_grouped())

    def __repr__(self):
        return "{}, grouped over {!r} \n{!r} groups with labels {}.".format(
            self.__class__.__name__,
            self._unique_coord.name,
            self._unique_coord.size,
            ", ".join(format_array_flat(self._unique_coord, 30).split()),
        )

    def _get_index_and_items(self, index, grouper):
        from .resample_cftime import CFTimeGrouper

        s = pd.Series(np.arange(index.size), index)
        if isinstance(grouper, CFTimeGrouper):
            first_items = grouper.first_items(index)
        else:
            first_items = s.groupby(grouper).first()
            _apply_loffset(grouper, first_items)
        full_index = first_items.index
        if first_items.isnull().any():
            first_items = first_items.dropna()
        return full_index, first_items

    def _iter_grouped(self):
        """Iterate over each element in this group"""
        for indices in self._group_indices:
            yield self._obj.isel(**{self._group_dim: indices})

    def _infer_concat_args(self, applied_example):
        if self._group_dim in applied_example.dims:
            coord = self._group
            positions = self._group_indices
        else:
            coord = self._unique_coord
            positions = None
        (dim,) = coord.dims
        if isinstance(coord, _DummyGroup):
            coord = None
        return coord, dim, positions

    @staticmethod
    def _binary_op(f, reflexive=False, **ignored_kwargs):
        @functools.wraps(f)
        def func(self, other):
            g = f if not reflexive else lambda x, y: f(y, x)
            applied = self._yield_binary_applied(g, other)
            combined = self._combine(applied)
            return combined

        return func

    def _yield_binary_applied(self, func, other):
        dummy = None

        for group_value, obj in self:
            try:
                other_sel = other.sel(**{self._group.name: group_value})
            except AttributeError:
                raise TypeError(
                    "GroupBy objects only support binary ops "
                    "when the other argument is a Dataset or "
                    "DataArray"
                )
            except (KeyError, ValueError):
                if self._group.name not in other.dims:
                    raise ValueError(
                        "incompatible dimensions for a grouped "
                        "binary operation: the group variable %r "
                        "is not a dimension on the other argument" % self._group.name
                    )
                if dummy is None:
                    dummy = _dummy_copy(other)
                other_sel = dummy

            result = func(obj, other_sel)
            yield result

    def _maybe_restore_empty_groups(self, combined):
        """Our index contained empty groups (e.g., from a resampling). If we
        reduced on that dimension, we want to restore the full index.
        """
        if self._full_index is not None and self._group.name in combined.dims:
            indexers = {self._group.name: self._full_index}
            combined = combined.reindex(**indexers)
        return combined

    def _maybe_unstack(self, obj):
        """This gets called if we are applying on an array with a
        multidimensional group."""
        if self._stacked_dim is not None and self._stacked_dim in obj.dims:
            obj = obj.unstack(self._stacked_dim)
            for dim in self._inserted_dims:
                if dim in obj.coords:
                    del obj.coords[dim]
            obj._indexes = propagate_indexes(obj._indexes, exclude=self._inserted_dims)
        return obj

    def fillna(self, value):
        """Fill missing values in this object by group.

        This operation follows the normal broadcasting and alignment rules that
        xarray uses for binary arithmetic, except the result is aligned to this
        object (``join='left'``) instead of aligned to the intersection of
        index coordinates (``join='inner'``).

        Parameters
        ----------
        value : valid type for the grouped object's fillna method
            Used to fill all matching missing values by group.

        Returns
        -------
        same type as the grouped object

        See also
        --------
        Dataset.fillna
        DataArray.fillna
        """
        out = ops.fillna(self, value)
        return out

    def quantile(
        self, q, dim=None, interpolation="linear", keep_attrs=None, skipna=True
    ):
        """Compute the qth quantile over each array in the groups and
        concatenate them together into a new array.

        Parameters
        ----------
        q : float in range of [0,1] (or sequence of floats)
            Quantile to compute, which must be between 0 and 1
            inclusive.
        dim : `...`, str or sequence of str, optional
            Dimension(s) over which to apply quantile.
            Defaults to the grouped dimension.
        interpolation : {'linear', 'lower', 'higher', 'midpoint', 'nearest'}
            This optional parameter specifies the interpolation method to
            use when the desired quantile lies between two data points
            ``i < j``:

                * linear: ``i + (j - i) * fraction``, where ``fraction`` is
                  the fractional part of the index surrounded by ``i`` and
                  ``j``.
                * lower: ``i``.
                * higher: ``j``.
                * nearest: ``i`` or ``j``, whichever is nearest.
                * midpoint: ``(i + j) / 2``.
        skipna : bool, optional
            Whether to skip missing values when aggregating.

        Returns
        -------
        quantiles : Variable
            If `q` is a single quantile, then the result is a
            scalar. If multiple percentiles are given, first axis of
            the result corresponds to the quantile. In either case a
            quantile dimension is added to the return array. The other
            dimensions are the dimensions that remain after the
            reduction of the array.

        See Also
        --------
        numpy.nanquantile, numpy.quantile, pandas.Series.quantile, Dataset.quantile,
        DataArray.quantile

        Examples
        --------

        >>> da = xr.DataArray(
        ...     [[1.3, 8.4, 0.7, 6.9], [0.7, 4.2, 9.4, 1.5], [6.5, 7.3, 2.6, 1.9]],
        ...     coords={"x": [0, 0, 1], "y": [1, 1, 2, 2]},
        ...     dims=("y", "y"),
        ... )
        >>> ds = xr.Dataset({"a": da})
        >>> da.groupby("x").quantile(0)
        <xarray.DataArray (x: 2, y: 4)>
        array([[0.7, 4.2, 0.7, 1.5],
               [6.5, 7.3, 2.6, 1.9]])
        Coordinates:
            quantile  float64 0.0
          * y         (y) int64 1 1 2 2
          * x         (x) int64 0 1
        >>> ds.groupby("y").quantile(0, dim=...)
        <xarray.Dataset>
        Dimensions:   (y: 2)
        Coordinates:
            quantile  float64 0.0
          * y         (y) int64 1 2
        Data variables:
            a         (y) float64 0.7 0.7
        >>> da.groupby("x").quantile([0, 0.5, 1])
        <xarray.DataArray (x: 2, y: 4, quantile: 3)>
        array([[[0.7 , 1.  , 1.3 ],
                [4.2 , 6.3 , 8.4 ],
                [0.7 , 5.05, 9.4 ],
                [1.5 , 4.2 , 6.9 ]],
               [[6.5 , 6.5 , 6.5 ],
                [7.3 , 7.3 , 7.3 ],
                [2.6 , 2.6 , 2.6 ],
                [1.9 , 1.9 , 1.9 ]]])
        Coordinates:
          * y         (y) int64 1 1 2 2
          * quantile  (quantile) float64 0.0 0.5 1.0
          * x         (x) int64 0 1
        >>> ds.groupby("y").quantile([0, 0.5, 1], dim=...)
        <xarray.Dataset>
        Dimensions:   (quantile: 3, y: 2)
        Coordinates:
          * quantile  (quantile) float64 0.0 0.5 1.0
          * y         (y) int64 1 2
        Data variables:
            a         (y, quantile) float64 0.7 5.35 8.4 0.7 2.25 9.4
        """
        if dim is None:
            dim = self._group_dim

        out = self.map(
            self._obj.__class__.quantile,
            shortcut=False,
            q=q,
            dim=dim,
            interpolation=interpolation,
            keep_attrs=keep_attrs,
            skipna=skipna,
        )

        return out

    def where(self, cond, other=dtypes.NA):
        """Return elements from `self` or `other` depending on `cond`.

        Parameters
        ----------
        cond : DataArray or Dataset with boolean dtype
            Locations at which to preserve this objects values.
        other : scalar, DataArray or Dataset, optional
            Value to use for locations in this object where ``cond`` is False.
            By default, inserts missing values.

        Returns
        -------
        same type as the grouped object

        See also
        --------
        Dataset.where
        """
        return ops.where_method(self, cond, other)

    def _first_or_last(self, op, skipna, keep_attrs):
        if isinstance(self._group_indices[0], integer_types):
            # NB. this is currently only used for reductions along an existing
            # dimension
            return self._obj
        if keep_attrs is None:
            keep_attrs = _get_keep_attrs(default=True)
        return self.reduce(op, self._group_dim, skipna=skipna, keep_attrs=keep_attrs)

    def first(self, skipna=None, keep_attrs=None):
        """Return the first element of each group along the group dimension
        """
        return self._first_or_last(duck_array_ops.first, skipna, keep_attrs)

    def last(self, skipna=None, keep_attrs=None):
        """Return the last element of each group along the group dimension
        """
        return self._first_or_last(duck_array_ops.last, skipna, keep_attrs)

    def assign_coords(self, coords=None, **coords_kwargs):
        """Assign coordinates by group.

        See also
        --------
        Dataset.assign_coords
        Dataset.swap_dims
        """
        coords_kwargs = either_dict_or_kwargs(coords, coords_kwargs, "assign_coords")
        return self.map(lambda ds: ds.assign_coords(**coords_kwargs))


def _maybe_reorder(xarray_obj, dim, positions):
    order = _inverse_permutation_indices(positions)

    if order is None or len(order) != xarray_obj.sizes[dim]:
        return xarray_obj
    else:
        return xarray_obj[{dim: order}]


class DataArrayGroupBy(GroupBy, ImplementsArrayReduce):
    """GroupBy object specialized to grouping DataArray objects
    """

    def _iter_grouped_shortcut(self):
        """Fast version of `_iter_grouped` that yields Variables without
        metadata
        """
        var = self._obj.variable
        for indices in self._group_indices:
            yield var[{self._group_dim: indices}]

    def _concat_shortcut(self, applied, dim, positions=None):
        # nb. don't worry too much about maintaining this method -- it does
        # speed things up, but it's not very interpretable and there are much
        # faster alternatives (e.g., doing the grouped aggregation in a
        # compiled language)
        stacked = Variable.concat(applied, dim, shortcut=True)
        reordered = _maybe_reorder(stacked, dim, positions)
        result = self._obj._replace_maybe_drop_dims(reordered)
        return result

    def _restore_dim_order(self, stacked):
        def lookup_order(dimension):
            if dimension == self._group.name:
                (dimension,) = self._group.dims
            if dimension in self._obj.dims:
                axis = self._obj.get_axis_num(dimension)
            else:
                axis = 1e6  # some arbitrarily high value
            return axis

        new_order = sorted(stacked.dims, key=lookup_order)
        return stacked.transpose(*new_order, transpose_coords=self._restore_coord_dims)

    def map(self, func, shortcut=False, args=(), **kwargs):
        """Apply a function to each array in the group and concatenate them
        together into a new array.

        `func` is called like `func(ar, *args, **kwargs)` for each array `ar`
        in this group.

        Apply uses heuristics (like `pandas.GroupBy.apply`) to figure out how
        to stack together the array. The rule is:

        1. If the dimension along which the group coordinate is defined is
           still in the first grouped array after applying `func`, then stack
           over this dimension.
        2. Otherwise, stack over the new dimension given by name of this
           grouping (the argument to the `groupby` function).

        Parameters
        ----------
        func : function
            Callable to apply to each array.
        shortcut : bool, optional
            Whether or not to shortcut evaluation under the assumptions that:

            (1) The action of `func` does not depend on any of the array
                metadata (attributes or coordinates) but only on the data and
                dimensions.
            (2) The action of `func` creates arrays with homogeneous metadata,
                that is, with the same dimensions and attributes.

            If these conditions are satisfied `shortcut` provides significant
            speedup. This should be the case for many common groupby operations
            (e.g., applying numpy ufuncs).
        ``*args`` : tuple, optional
            Positional arguments passed to `func`.
        ``**kwargs``
            Used to call `func(ar, **kwargs)` for each array `ar`.

        Returns
        -------
        applied : DataArray or DataArray
            The result of splitting, applying and combining this array.
        """
        if shortcut:
            grouped = self._iter_grouped_shortcut()
        else:
            grouped = self._iter_grouped()
        applied = (maybe_wrap_array(arr, func(arr, *args, **kwargs)) for arr in grouped)
        return self._combine(applied, shortcut=shortcut)

    def apply(self, func, shortcut=False, args=(), **kwargs):
        """
        Backward compatible implementation of ``map``

        See Also
        --------
        DataArrayGroupBy.map
        """
        warnings.warn(
            "GroupBy.apply may be deprecated in the future. Using GroupBy.map is encouraged",
            PendingDeprecationWarning,
            stacklevel=2,
        )
        return self.map(func, shortcut=shortcut, args=args, **kwargs)

    def _combine(self, applied, restore_coord_dims=False, shortcut=False):
        """Recombine the applied objects like the original."""
        applied_example, applied = peek_at(applied)
        coord, dim, positions = self._infer_concat_args(applied_example)
        if shortcut:
            combined = self._concat_shortcut(applied, dim, positions)
        else:
            combined = concat(applied, dim)
            combined = _maybe_reorder(combined, dim, positions)

        if isinstance(combined, type(self._obj)):
            # only restore dimension order for arrays
            combined = self._restore_dim_order(combined)
        # assign coord when the applied function does not return that coord
        if coord is not None and dim not in applied_example.dims:
            if shortcut:
                coord_var = as_variable(coord)
                combined._coords[coord.name] = coord_var
            else:
                combined.coords[coord.name] = coord
        combined = self._maybe_restore_empty_groups(combined)
        combined = self._maybe_unstack(combined)
        return combined

    def reduce(
        self, func, dim=None, axis=None, keep_attrs=None, shortcut=True, **kwargs
    ):
        """Reduce the items in this group by applying `func` along some
        dimension(s).

        Parameters
        ----------
        func : function
            Function which can be called in the form
            `func(x, axis=axis, **kwargs)` to return the result of collapsing
            an np.ndarray over an integer valued axis.
        dim : `...`, str or sequence of str, optional
            Dimension(s) over which to apply `func`.
        axis : int or sequence of int, optional
            Axis(es) over which to apply `func`. Only one of the 'dimension'
            and 'axis' arguments can be supplied. If neither are supplied, then
            `func` is calculated over all dimension for each group item.
        keep_attrs : bool, optional
            If True, the datasets's attributes (`attrs`) will be copied from
            the original object to the new one.  If False (default), the new
            object will be returned without attributes.
        **kwargs : dict
            Additional keyword arguments passed on to `func`.

        Returns
        -------
        reduced : Array
            Array with summarized data and the indicated dimension(s)
            removed.
        """
        if dim is None:
            dim = self._group_dim

        if keep_attrs is None:
            keep_attrs = _get_keep_attrs(default=False)

        def reduce_array(ar):
            return ar.reduce(func, dim, axis, keep_attrs=keep_attrs, **kwargs)

        check_reduce_dims(dim, self.dims)

        return self.map(reduce_array, shortcut=shortcut)


ops.inject_reduce_methods(DataArrayGroupBy)
ops.inject_binary_ops(DataArrayGroupBy)


class DatasetGroupBy(GroupBy, ImplementsDatasetReduce):
    def map(self, func, args=(), shortcut=None, **kwargs):
        """Apply a function to each Dataset in the group and concatenate them
        together into a new Dataset.

        `func` is called like `func(ds, *args, **kwargs)` for each dataset `ds`
        in this group.

        Apply uses heuristics (like `pandas.GroupBy.apply`) to figure out how
        to stack together the datasets. The rule is:

        1. If the dimension along which the group coordinate is defined is
           still in the first grouped item after applying `func`, then stack
           over this dimension.
        2. Otherwise, stack over the new dimension given by name of this
           grouping (the argument to the `groupby` function).

        Parameters
        ----------
        func : function
            Callable to apply to each sub-dataset.
        args : tuple, optional
            Positional arguments to pass to `func`.
        **kwargs
            Used to call `func(ds, **kwargs)` for each sub-dataset `ar`.

        Returns
        -------
        applied : Dataset or DataArray
            The result of splitting, applying and combining this dataset.
        """
        # ignore shortcut if set (for now)
        applied = (func(ds, *args, **kwargs) for ds in self._iter_grouped())
        return self._combine(applied)

    def apply(self, func, args=(), shortcut=None, **kwargs):
        """
        Backward compatible implementation of ``map``

        See Also
        --------
        DatasetGroupBy.map
        """

        warnings.warn(
            "GroupBy.apply may be deprecated in the future. Using GroupBy.map is encouraged",
            PendingDeprecationWarning,
            stacklevel=2,
        )
        return self.map(func, shortcut=shortcut, args=args, **kwargs)

    def _combine(self, applied):
        """Recombine the applied objects like the original."""
        applied_example, applied = peek_at(applied)
        coord, dim, positions = self._infer_concat_args(applied_example)
        combined = concat(applied, dim)
        combined = _maybe_reorder(combined, dim, positions)
        # assign coord when the applied function does not return that coord
        if coord is not None and dim not in applied_example.dims:
            combined[coord.name] = coord
        combined = self._maybe_restore_empty_groups(combined)
        combined = self._maybe_unstack(combined)
        return combined

    def reduce(self, func, dim=None, keep_attrs=None, **kwargs):
        """Reduce the items in this group by applying `func` along some
        dimension(s).

        Parameters
        ----------
        func : function
            Function which can be called in the form
            `func(x, axis=axis, **kwargs)` to return the result of collapsing
            an np.ndarray over an integer valued axis.
        dim : `...`, str or sequence of str, optional
            Dimension(s) over which to apply `func`.
        axis : int or sequence of int, optional
            Axis(es) over which to apply `func`. Only one of the 'dimension'
            and 'axis' arguments can be supplied. If neither are supplied, then
            `func` is calculated over all dimension for each group item.
        keep_attrs : bool, optional
            If True, the datasets's attributes (`attrs`) will be copied from
            the original object to the new one.  If False (default), the new
            object will be returned without attributes.
        **kwargs : dict
            Additional keyword arguments passed on to `func`.

        Returns
        -------
        reduced : Array
            Array with summarized data and the indicated dimension(s)
            removed.
        """
        if dim is None:
            dim = self._group_dim

        if keep_attrs is None:
            keep_attrs = _get_keep_attrs(default=False)

        def reduce_dataset(ds):
            return ds.reduce(func, dim, keep_attrs, **kwargs)

        check_reduce_dims(dim, self.dims)

        return self.map(reduce_dataset)

    def assign(self, **kwargs):
        """Assign data variables by group.

        See also
        --------
        Dataset.assign
        """
        return self.map(lambda ds: ds.assign(**kwargs))


ops.inject_reduce_methods(DatasetGroupBy)
ops.inject_binary_ops(DatasetGroupBy)

        ...     },
        ...     coords={"lat": [10, 20], "lon": [150, 160]},
        ... )
        >>> x
        <xarray.Dataset>
        Dimensions:        (lat: 2, lon: 2)
        Coordinates:
        * lat            (lat) int64 10 20
        * lon            (lon) int64 150 160
        Data variables:
            temperature_c  (lat, lon) float64 14.53 11.85 19.27 16.37
            precipitation  (lat, lon) float64 0.7315 0.7189 0.8481 0.4671

        >>> def adder(data, arg):
        ...     return data + arg
        ...
        >>> def div(data, arg):
        ...     return data / arg
        ...
        >>> def sub_mult(data, sub_arg, mult_arg):
        ...     return (data * mult_arg) - sub_arg
        ...
        >>> x.pipe(adder, 2)
        <xarray.Dataset>
        Dimensions:        (lat: 2, lon: 2)
        Coordinates:
        * lon            (lon) int64 150 160
        * lat            (lat) int64 10 20
        Data variables:
            temperature_c  (lat, lon) float64 16.53 13.85 21.27 18.37
            precipitation  (lat, lon) float64 2.731 2.719 2.848 2.467

        >>> x.pipe(adder, arg=2)
        <xarray.Dataset>
        Dimensions:        (lat: 2, lon: 2)
        Coordinates:
        * lon            (lon) int64 150 160
        * lat            (lat) int64 10 20
        Data variables:
            temperature_c  (lat, lon) float64 16.53 13.85 21.27 18.37
            precipitation  (lat, lon) float64 2.731 2.719 2.848 2.467

        >>> (
        ...     x.pipe(adder, arg=2)
        ...     .pipe(div, arg=2)
        ...     .pipe(sub_mult, sub_arg=2, mult_arg=2)
        ... )
        <xarray.Dataset>
        Dimensions:        (lat: 2, lon: 2)
        Coordinates:
        * lon            (lon) int64 150 160
        * lat            (lat) int64 10 20
        Data variables:
            temperature_c  (lat, lon) float64 14.53 11.85 19.27 16.37
            precipitation  (lat, lon) float64 0.7315 0.7189 0.8481 0.4671

        See Also
        --------
        pandas.DataFrame.pipe
        """
        if isinstance(func, tuple):
            func, target = func
            if target in kwargs:
                raise ValueError(
                    "%s is both the pipe target and a keyword " "argument" % target
                )
            kwargs[target] = self
            return func(*args, **kwargs)
        else:
            return func(self, *args, **kwargs)

import functools
import warnings
from typing import Any, Callable, Dict

import numpy as np

from . import dtypes, duck_array_ops, utils
from .dask_array_ops import dask_rolling_wrapper
from .ops import inject_reduce_methods
from .options import _get_keep_attrs
from .pycompat import dask_array_type

try:
    import bottleneck
except ImportError:
    # use numpy methods instead
    bottleneck = None


_ROLLING_REDUCE_DOCSTRING_TEMPLATE = """\
Reduce this object's data windows by applying `{name}` along its dimension.

Parameters
----------
**kwargs : dict
    Additional keyword arguments passed on to `{name}`.

Returns
-------
reduced : same type as caller
    New object with `{name}` applied along its rolling dimnension.
"""


class Rolling:
    """A object that implements the moving window pattern.

    See Also
    --------
    Dataset.groupby
    DataArray.groupby
    Dataset.rolling
    DataArray.rolling
    """

    __slots__ = ("obj", "window", "min_periods", "center", "dim", "keep_attrs")
    _attributes = ("window", "min_periods", "center", "dim", "keep_attrs")

    def __init__(self, obj, windows, min_periods=None, center=False, keep_attrs=None):
        """
        Moving window object.

        Parameters
        ----------
        obj : Dataset or DataArray
            Object to window.
        windows : A mapping from a dimension name to window size
            dim : str
                Name of the dimension to create the rolling iterator
                along (e.g., `time`).
            window : int
                Size of the moving window.
        min_periods : int, default None
            Minimum number of observations in window required to have a value
            (otherwise result is NA). The default, None, is equivalent to
            setting min_periods equal to the size of the window.
        center : boolean, default False
            Set the labels at the center of the window.
        keep_attrs : bool, optional
            If True, the object's attributes (`attrs`) will be copied from
            the original object to the new one.  If False (default), the new
            object will be returned without attributes.

        Returns
        -------
        rolling : type of input argument
        """
        if len(windows) != 1:
            raise ValueError("exactly one dim/window should be provided")

        dim, window = next(iter(windows.items()))

        if window <= 0:
            raise ValueError("window must be > 0")

        self.obj = obj

        # attributes
        self.window = window
        if min_periods is not None and min_periods <= 0:
            raise ValueError("min_periods must be greater than zero or None")
        self.min_periods = min_periods

        self.center = center
        self.dim = dim

        if keep_attrs is None:
            keep_attrs = _get_keep_attrs(default=False)
        self.keep_attrs = keep_attrs

    @property
    def _min_periods(self):
        return self.min_periods if self.min_periods is not None else self.window

    def __repr__(self):
        """provide a nice str repr of our rolling object"""

        attrs = [
            "{k}->{v}".format(k=k, v=getattr(self, k))
            for k in self._attributes
            if getattr(self, k, None) is not None
        ]
        return "{klass} [{attrs}]".format(
            klass=self.__class__.__name__, attrs=",".join(attrs)
        )

    def __len__(self):
        return self.obj.sizes[self.dim]

    def _reduce_method(name: str) -> Callable:  # type: ignore
        array_agg_func = getattr(duck_array_ops, name)
        bottleneck_move_func = getattr(bottleneck, "move_" + name, None)

        def method(self, **kwargs):
            return self._numpy_or_bottleneck_reduce(
                array_agg_func, bottleneck_move_func, **kwargs
            )

        method.__name__ = name
        method.__doc__ = _ROLLING_REDUCE_DOCSTRING_TEMPLATE.format(name=name)
        return method

    argmax = _reduce_method("argmax")
    argmin = _reduce_method("argmin")
    max = _reduce_method("max")
    min = _reduce_method("min")
    mean = _reduce_method("mean")
    prod = _reduce_method("prod")
    sum = _reduce_method("sum")
    std = _reduce_method("std")
    var = _reduce_method("var")
    median = _reduce_method("median")

    def count(self):
        rolling_count = self._counts()
        enough_periods = rolling_count >= self._min_periods
        return rolling_count.where(enough_periods)

    count.__doc__ = _ROLLING_REDUCE_DOCSTRING_TEMPLATE.format(name="count")


class DataArrayRolling(Rolling):

    def __init__(self, obj, windows, min_periods=None, center=False, keep_attrs=None):
        """
        Moving window object for DataArray.
        You should use DataArray.rolling() method to construct this object
        instead of the class constructor.

        Parameters
        ----------
        obj : DataArray
            Object to window.

            dim : str
                Name of the dimension to create the rolling iterator
                along (e.g., `time`).
            window : int
                Size of the moving window.
        min_periods : int, default None
            Minimum number of observations in window required to have a value
            (otherwise result is NA). The default, None, is equivalent to
            setting min_periods equal to the size of the window.
        center : boolean, default False
            Set the labels at the center of the window.
        keep_attrs : bool, optional
            If True, the object's attributes (`attrs`) will be copied from
            the original object to the new one.  If False (default), the new
            object will be returned without attributes.
